entry.title: Sales area info not updaing after adding custom tab on Business Partner
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I have added a custom tab to the Business Partner (BP) transaction in S/4HANA.

I created the tab using the BDT (BUS* transactions) and a Z function group with a custom subscreen containing a table control.

Everything works except for one issue:

My problem is that, when I navigate to my custom tab the standard Sales Area fields in the BP header stop updating correctly (visually).

Here the different scenarios:


1- When I visualize a client starting on some standard tab everything is OK and I can change the sales area.

2- If I go now to my custom tab 'GrypoVend - Marca' the sales area information remains OK. But when I try to change it to another sales org:
Text belonging to the fields change but not the codes.

3- When BP starts directly on my custom tab this Sales area information is empty so I choose one. When I do it so, happens de same as before:
No codes but it does update the description

I want to update the codes too so it all have sense and its correct for the user who sees it. When I debug I get through 
 cvi_bdt_adapter=>get_current_bp_sales_data(
   EXPORTING
   i_table_name = 'KNVV'
   IMPORTING
   e_data_table = gt_knvv[]
   ).

the correct sales area information, but I still want to show it on BP.

Can anyone help me with it or give an explanation/solution?

Regards.

entry.title: Confirm the minimum version of OnPrem S4HANA version required for BRIM Quote Replication iflow
entry.content:
There has been a breaking change for CPQ 2608 regarding the BRIM integration on the iFlow "Replicate Quote 2.0 from SAP CPQ to SAP S/4HANA "

As per the help documentation - https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_CPQ/abe5bf645c9542a5bd4cbfdcc1a4876c/8e920b7b0a2e4d7fa72c306194818522.html, the new iflow "New Replicate Quote 2.0 from SAP CPQ to SAP S/4HANA " will become the default and the old iflow will be deprecated.

After checking the documentation on CPQ BRIM integration, in this page

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_CPQ/a5de4dd4b8b047feb894c618aed793b9/89cca9325a704bd79c39299617c030eb.html

 

It is stated that the minimum version required SAP S/4HANA On-premise release required is OP2023 FPS2.  

But our OnPrem system is on 2021. And S/4 HANA upgrade isn't planned by our Stream leads for 2026 at least.

Could you please confirm if the minimum version of OnPrem S4 HANA version required is correct for the new CPQ BRIMflow?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Confirm the minimum version of OnPrem S4HANA version...
entry.content:
CPQ-BRIM Integration is supported from OP2021 onwards.. In CPQ-BRIM Integration Tiered Pricing feature is supported from OP2023 FPS2 onwards.

The same is present in the help documentation: Setup Tiered Pricing Procedure in SAP S/4HANA | SAP Help Portal

It says here "The SAP S/4HANA will support tiered pricing feature starting from the OP2023 FPS2 release."

 

And in this page System Requirements | SAP Help Portal it says "SAP Billing Revenue and Innovation Management – minimum version 2021 FPS1 release".

entry.title: Re: CLM - Contract and Lease Management Migration in Publ...
entry.content:
Hello @SonyaM

I would not say “this should never exist in any IFRS16 cash flow at any point in time” as a universal accounting statement. I would say that for this migration approach, using Present Value Specified, the setup is not accepted by valuation logic once the specified present value exceeds the remaining contract value used for valuation.

When I said you should verify whether NPV amount was loaded incorrectly, or whether the contract value basis is incomplete, what I meant is that one of these two things may be true:

  1. The liability closing amount taken from RE-FX was loaded correctly, but the remaining lease payments in CLM are incomplete. For example, one or more future lease payment cash flows are missing, or payment term dates are incomplete after cutover. In that case, the contract value in CLM is understated, so the specific present value looks too high only because of the future payments basis is incomplete.
  2. The remaining lease payments in CLM are correct, but the Present Value Specified you imported is not the right value to use for this transition setup. In that case, the Present Value Specified is overstated for the CLM contract basis, even if it matched a report in the legacy system.

That is why reconciliation to legacy is not enough by itself. For the system to valuate the migrated CLM contract successfully, you must align remaining future lease payments in CLM, Present Value Specified, and Asset Default Value. If the liability brought from RE-FX was build on a broader payment basis than the one now represented in CLM, you will get exactly this inconsistency.  

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3bd9b5b7e36c4628a29ffc464bf69b48/44341a9cb5c74d4dbd3e78b473439ca8.html?locale=en-US

3653143 - Central KBA: Interest Calculation in RE-FX - SAP for Me

On note 3409502 and OBA5, (which is a classic GUI transaction) your observation is correct. If a note requires OBA5, that part is not applicable in Public Cloud, because that transaction is not available there. So, I would not position 3409502 as a practical Public Cloud recommended path for your case.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Currency Conversion Integration using SAP CPI with Groovy and XSLT
entry.content:
Introduction

In many real-world integration scenarios, applications need to handle data across different currencies. Whether it’s for reporting, billing, or analytics, converting currency values accurately and in real time is an important requirement.

In this blog, I’ll walk through how I built a simple and practical currency conversion integration using SAP Cloud Integration (CPI). The iFlow accepts a JSON request, calls an external API to fetch exchange rates, processes the response using Groovy, and transforms the output into a clean, readable format using XSLT. Finally, the result is sent as an email, making it easy for end users to view.

 

Integration Flow Overview

This integration flow performs the following:

  • Accepts a currency conversion request in JSON format
  • Calls an external exchange rate API
  • Converts the source amount into target currency
  • Transforms the response into a readable HTML format
  • Sends the final output via Email

 

Integration Flow Diagram

HTTPS Sender → JSON to XML Converter → Content Modifier (Extract Properties) → Request Reply (External API Call) → Groovy Script (Processing) → Content Modifier (Build XML) → XSLT Mapping (HTML Output) → Email Receiver

Below is the iFlow implemented for this scenario:

The iFlow demonstrates message splitting, dynamic routing, and CSV transformation before sending data to respective receivers.

Input Payload Structure

Step 1: JSON to XML Conversion

The incoming JSON payload is converted into XML to enable XPath-based extraction in subsequent steps.

Step 2: Extracting Properties 

Using Content Modifier, the following fields are extracted:

 

 
 
These are stored as exchange properties for reuse.

Step 3: External API Call 

A Request Reply step is used to call the exchange rate API dynamically:

 Step 4: Groovy Script Processing

The API response is processed using Groovy to extract relevant fields and format the converted amount.

 Step 5: Building Output XML

A structured XML is created:

 Step 6: XSLT Transformation

The XML is transformed into an HTML table using XSLT to make the output readable.

 Step 7: Email Notification

 The final formatted result is sent via Email using the Mail adapter.

 

 Challenges Faced 

  • Handling API authentication issues
  • Parsing dynamic JSON responses
  • Resolving null pointer exceptions
  • Ensuring correct data formatting

 

Key Learnings

  • Importance of validating API responses
  • Effective use of Groovy for JSON processing
  • Combining XML and XSLT for formatting
  • Designing efficient CPI iFlows

 

Conclusion

This use case demonstrates how SAP CPI can be leveraged to build real-time integrations involving external APIs, data transformation, and user-friendly output delivery.

This implementation helped me better understand how to design flexible and maintainable integration flows, especially when dealing with real-world requirements.

I hope this experience helps others who are working on similar scenarios in SAP Cloud Integration.

Happy to hear feedback or suggestions from the community.

entry.title: Architecting a Secure API Bridge: Automating SAP Datasphere Data Consumption via OAuth 2.0/Postman
entry.content:
The Strategic Need for an API Bridge

In a modern enterprise, SAP Datasphere acts as the "Single Source of Truth." However, the data stored there often needs to be consumed by diverse downstream consumers:  from custom Python-based AI models and React web applications to third-party reporting engines.

The challenge isn't just "getting the data out"—it’s doing so securely and efficiently. Relying on manual exports or basic authentication creates security risks and operational bottlenecks. By architecting an API Bridge using Postman, I created a standardized, automated, and OAuth 2.0-secured gateway. This allows us to move away from "one-off" data pulls and toward a professional Data-as-a-Service (DaaS) model where any authorized application can consume real-time SAP data on demand.

The blog is divided into 5 parts:

  • Part 1: The Data Foundation (Datasphere - Data Builder)

  • Part 2: Security & Identity—The OAuth 2.0 Client

  •  Part 3: The Connection (Postman Strategy)

    • Option 1: The Manual Approach (Standard)

    • Option 2: The Automated Approach (Professional)

  • Part 4: Real-Time Data Validation (The Visualizer)

  • Part 5: The End Goal—Exporting for Downstream Apps

Part 1: The Data Foundation (Datasphere - Data Builder)

Before an API can "see" your data, it must be properly modeled and exposed. For this setup, I used a dataset of Amazon Bestselling Books.

Why use a Graphical View instead of a Table?

While you can expose a raw table, a Graphical View is the professional choice. It acts as a "Virtual Layer" where you can:

  • Filter Data: Exclude old records so the API doesn't get overloaded.
  • Rename Fields: Change technical headers (like PUB_YR) to business-friendly names (like Publication_Year).
  • Calculations: Add new columns (like Profit_Margin) directly in the view.

Step-by-Step:

  1. Import: Upload your CSV/Table into the Data Builder.
  2. Create View: Drag the table into a new Graphical View.
  3. Semantic Usage: Set this to Relational Dataset (this is mandatory for OData access).
  4. Expose for Consumption: Toggle this ON. This is the most critical step; it tells Datasphere to generate a URL for external tools.
  5. Deploy: Click the "Save and Deploy" icon.

SAP Datasphere Data Builder view properties showing Expose for Consumption toggled on.

 

In the SAP Datasphere Data Builder, ensure your Graphical View is set to 'Relational Dataset' and that 'Expose for Consumption' is toggled ON. A successful deployment (indicated by the green checkmark) is required for the OData service to become reachable.

Part 2: Security & Identity—The OAuth 2.0 Client

To allow Postman to talk to SAP Datasphere, avoid using personal login credentials. Instead, create an OAuth 2.0 Client. This acts as a "Technical User" that is secure, scoped, and easy to manage.

Step 1: Navigate to App Integration

Go to System > Administration > App Integration tab.

SAP Datasphere Data Builder screen showing Model Properties for Bestsellers Books View with Relational Dataset usage and Expose for Consumption toggled ON.

 

Step 2: Create the Technical User

Click on "Add an OAuth Client". In the configuration window:

  1. Provide a Name (e.g., POC_Technical_User).
  2. Set the Authorization Grant to Client Credentials.
  3. Important: Copy your Secret immediately and save it safely; you won't be able to see it again!

Configuration window for an SAP Datasphere OAuth Client showing Name, Client ID field, and the Client Credentials authorization grant type.

 

Step 3: The "403" Safeguard (Role Assignment)

This is the most critical technical step. The API will return a 403 Forbidden error unless permissions are set. Click Select Roles and assign Scoped Data Warehouse Cloud Viewer and Scoped Data Warehouse Cloud Modeler as optional.

Role assignment screen in SAP Datasphere, selecting Scoped Data Warehouse Cloud Modeler and Scoped Data Warehouse Cloud Viewer roles for an OAuth client.

 

Step 4: Note your Endpoints

Once saved, your new client will appear in the Configured Clients list. Make sure to note down the Authorization URL and Token URL shown at the top of the screen. You will need these for your Postman Environment.

SAP Datasphere App Integration screen displaying the configured OAuth Clients list, Authorization URL, and Token URL.

 

 Part 3: The Connection (Postman Strategy)

Now that our SAP Datasphere "Bridge" is built and secured, we need to cross it using Postman. There are two ways to handle the OAuth 2.0 handshake.

Option 1: The Manual Approach (Standard)

While automation is our ultimate goal, establishing a manual connection first is a critical diagnostic step. This ensures that your SAP Datasphere credentials and roles are configured correctly before we add the complexity of scripting. This is the "out-of-the-box" method. It is great for a quick one-time test, but it has a significant drawback: Tokens expire.

Detailed Steps:

Step 1: Configure the Request

In Postman, create a new GET request using your Datasphere OData URL. Navigate to the Authorization tab and select OAuth 2.0. Fill in the "Configure New Token" section using the Client ID and Secret we generated in Part 2.

Postman Authorization tab setup for OAuth 2.0 showing Client ID and Client Secret fields highlighted.

 

Step 2: Authenticate with SAP

Scroll down and click "Get New Access Token". If your credentials are correct, you will see a green checkmark indicating that the authentication handshake was successful.

Postman popup window showing a green checkmark and 'Authentication complete' message.

 

Step 3: Commit the Token

Once authenticated, Postman stores the token in its local manager. You must click "Use Token" to link this specific string to your current request headers.

Postman Manage Access Tokens window highlighting the 'Use Token' button.

 

Headers: Go to the Headers tab. You must add the key x-sap-sac-custom-auth with the value true. This tells the SAP gateway to process your request as a technical user.

 

Step 4: Execute the Call

With the token active, you are ready to hit Send. Postman will now pass the bearer token to SAP Datasphere to request your data.

Postman interface highlighting the blue 'Send' button for the OData request.

Step 5: Validate the Result

A successful connection will return a 200 OK status. You can now see your Bestsellers Books data in raw JSON format. While this confirms the "Bridge" is working, notice how hard it is to read—this is why we will implement the Visualizer in Part 4.

Postman response body showing 200 OK status and raw JSON data for the Amazon Bestseller Books dataset.

The Comparison (Transitioning to Option 2)

The manual method works perfectly, but as you can see, it requires several clicks. If you close Postman or wait an hour, that token will expire, and you will have to repeat these steps.

Next, let's explore how to automate this entire flow using a Pre-request Script, so you never have to click "Get New Token" again.

Option 2: The Automated Approach (Professional)

While the manual approach confirms our connection works, it is not scalable. For a truly professional API Bridge, we need a "Set-and-Forget" solution. By using a Pre-request Script, we turn Postman into an intelligent agent that manages its own OAuth 2.0 lifecycle. This is the "Set-and-Forget" method. We use a Pre-request Script to turn Postman into an intelligent agent that manages its own security.

Step 1: Establishing the Environment Layer

Before we write the script, we must centralize our configuration. Instead of hardcoding URLs and Secrets—which is a security risk and an administrative nightmare—we use Postman Environments.

As shown in the image below, create a new environment and name it (e.g., SAP Datasphere). This allows us to store our ds_url, client_id, and token_url as variables.

Postman Environment tab showing variables for ds_url, client_id, client_secret, token_url, and automated fields for access_token and token_expiry.

Why this matters:

  • Security: Your client_secret is masked.
  • Automation: Notice the access_token and token_expiry variables. Our script will automatically update these values every time a new handshake occurs, keeping the connection "alive" indefinitely.

Step 2: The Pre-request Script (The Brain)

Instead of adding a script to every individual request, we add it to the Collection Folder. This ensures the logic applies to every OData call you ever add to this project.

  1. Click the three dots next to your "Datasphere Connection" collection and select Edit.
  2. Navigate to the Scripts > Pre-request tab.
  3. The Logic: The script checks if a token exists or if the current time has passed the token_expiry. If needed, it fetches a fresh token silently in the background.

Paste this script into the Collection Pre-request tab:

// Check if the token is expired (or doesn't exist)

if (!pm.environment.get("access_token") || Date.now() > pm.environment.get("token_expiry")) {
    console.log("Token expired or missing. Fetching a new one...");
    pm.sendRequest({
        url: pm.environment.get("token_url"),
        method: 'POST',
        header: {
            'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded',
            'Authorization': 'Basic ' + btoa(pm.environment.get("client_id") + ":" + pm.environment.get("client_secret"))
        },
        body: {
            mode: 'urlencoded',
            urlencoded: [
                { key: 'grant_type', value: 'client_credentials' }
            ]
        }
    }, function (err, res) {
        if (err) {
            console.log("Error fetching token: " + err);
        } else {
            var response = res.json();
            pm.environment.set("access_token", response.access_token);
 
// Set expiry time (current time + expires_in seconds)

            pm.environment.set("token_expiry", Date.now() + (response.expires_in * 1000));
            console.log("New token fetched successfully!");
        }
    });
}

 

Postman Pre-request script showing advanced OAuth 2.0 logic with automated expiration checking and environment variable management.

Click the Save button at the top right of the Collection tab (or press Ctrl+S). If you don't save here, the collection won't "know" to run this logic before your requests.

Step 3: Inherit Authentication

After the Pre-request script fetches a fresh token and saves it to the environment, we need to tell our specific API request to use that value.

  • Authorization: On your GET request, set the Auth Type to Bearer Token.
  • Variable: In the Token field, type {{access_token}}.

           Postman interface showing the 'Bearer Token' authorization type and the reference to the access_token variable.

  • Environment: Ensure the "SAP Datasphere" environment is active in the top-right dropdown.Selecting the correct Environment in the top-right dropdown.
  • Headers Tab: Add the mandatory key 'x-sap-sac-custom-auth' and set it to 'true'. This is essential for both approaches to bypass the SAP login screen.

           Postman Headers tab showing the manually added key set to true, necessary for bypassing interactive SAP login screens during API calls.

  • Send: Click the blue Send button.

What happens when you click Send?

This is the "Grand Opening." When you hit Send, Postman follows this sequence:

  1. The Script Runs: It checks the environment variables. If the token is missing/expired, it calls the SAP Token URL.
  2. Variables Update: You’ll actually see the access_token and token_expiry values change in your Environment tab in real-time.
  3. The GET Request Fires: Postman injects the fresh token into the header and sends the request to Datasphere.
  4. The Result: You receive your 200 OK and the JSON data.

Postman response footer showing a successful 200 OK status code and response time, confirming a secure connection between Postman and SAP Datasphere.

Why this is the "Professional" Way:

This setup creates a seamless link. When you hit Send, Postman follows this sequence:

  1. The Script Runs: It checks the environment variables. If the token is missing/expired, it calls the SAP Token URL.
  2. Variables Update: You’ll actually see the access_token and token_expiry values change in your Environment tab in real-time.
  3. The GET Request Fires: Postman injects the fresh token into the header and sends the request to Datasphere.
  4. The Result: You receive a 200 OK response and the corresponding JSON data.

Benefits:

  • The Automated Advantage: You can walk away for a week, come back, hit "Send," and it will just work. The script handles the handshake before you even realize a token was needed.
  • Self-Healing: If the token expires, the script detects it and fixes it in milliseconds.
  • Security: Your secrets are stored in the Environment layer, not the request itself.
  • Scalability: You can add 100 different OData requests to this collection, and they will all share this automated login logic.

Part 4: Real-Time Data Validation (The Visualizer)

Raw JSON data is hard to read. An alternative option is to use the Postman Visualizer to transform our live SAP Datasphere OData feed into a professional, searchable dashboard.

The "Visualize" Tab:

Step 1: The "Post-Response" Script

In your automated GET request, navigate to the Scripts > Post-response tab. Unlike the Pre-request script, which handles security before the call, this script executes after the data arrives.

Paste the following code to map your OData fields to an HTML table (You can modify the template below as per your need):

// Define the HTML/CSS Template

var template = `
    <style>
        body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin: 20px; }
        #search { margin-bottom: 15px; padding: 10px; width: 100%; border: 1px solid #ccc; border-radius: 4px; }
        table { border-collapse: collapse; width: 100%; }
        th { background-color: #0070f3; color: white; padding: 10px; text-align: left; position: sticky; top: 0; }
        td { border: 1px solid #ddd; padding: 8px; }
        tr:nth-child(even) { background-color: #f2f2f2; }
        tr:hover { background-color: #e9f5ff; }
    </style>
    <input type="text" id="search" onkeyup="searchTable()" placeholder="Search for book names or authors...">
    <table id="bookTable">
        <thead>
            <tr>
                <th>Name</th>
                <th>Author</th>
                <th>Price</th>
                <th>Year</th>
            </tr>
        </thead>
        <tbody>
            {{#each response.value}}
            <tr>
                <td>{{Name}}</td>
                <td>{{Author}}</td>
                <td>{{Price}}</td>
                <td>{{Year}}</td>
            </tr>
            {{/each}}
        </tbody>
    </table>
    <script>
        function searchTable() {
            var input = document.getElementById("search");
            var filter = input.value.toUpperCase();
            var table = document.getElementById("bookTable");
            var tr = table.getElementsByTagName("tr");
            for (var i = 1; i < tr.length; i++) {
                var txtValue = tr[i].textContent || tr[i].innerText;
                if (txtValue.toUpperCase().indexOf(filter) > -1) {
                    tr[i].style.display = "";
                } else {
                    tr[i].style.display = "none";
                }
            }
        }
    </script>
`;

pm.visualizer.set(template, {
    response: pm.response.json()
});

Step 2: The "Grand Opening" (Click Send)

Once your script is saved, click the blue Send button.

  1. Status Check: You will see the 200 OK status return in the bottom right.
  2. Visualize: In the response body section, click the Visualize tab.
  3. Result: Your raw data is now a clean, interactive blue table. You can search for specific authors or titles in real-time.

Postman interface showing the final visualized OData table with a search bar, 200 OK status, and professional blue header formatting.

Part 5: The End Goal—Exporting for Downstream Apps

The ultimate goal of this "Bridge" is to feed other systems without manual intervention.

  • For Business Users: Use Postman’s "Send and Download" feature (click the arrow next to the Send button). This exports the live OData response into a .json or .csv file that can be ingested by Excel or Power BI.

  • For App Developers: Since the OAuth flow is now automated, this Postman Collection can be exported as a JSON file. Developers can use the Postman Collection Runner or Newman to integrate this data feed directly into a CI/CD pipeline or a custom React/Python application.

Conclusion: From Data Silo to API Bridge

In this guide, we didn't just connect Postman to SAP Datasphere—we built a production-ready OData bridge.

  • Exposed: Turned a static View into a live OData service.
  • Secured: Implemented robust OAuth 2.0 scoping.
  • Automated: Used Pre-request scripts to ensure the connection never expires.
  • Validated: Used the Visualizer to prove the data is accurate at a glance.

By following this workflow, you can empower your external applications, third-party vendors, or internal analytics tools with real-time data from your SAP ecosystem—securely and automatically.

#SAPDatasphere #SAPBDC #Postman #OAuth2 #OData #Integration #DataAsAService

entry.title: Dynamic Opening and Closing Balance Calculation in SAP Analytics Cloud Using Script Logic
entry.content:
Introduction 

In SAP Analytics Cloud particularly in financial and treasury reporting often we have a most common requirement to display opening and closing balance based on selected period.

Mostly the requirement will be done by manually adjusting the reporting period or by creating multiple static filters , but in this blog we are going to tackle the requirement by using SAC scripting and input controls. By Filtering the periods before and up to the selected period , SAC can automatically calculate the opening and closing balance and display it in the dashboard.

This blog explains how SAC scripting is used to dynamically show the opening and closing balances based on selected period.

 Business Scenario

The requirement is that the users should view treasury balances based on the selected period. The exact scenario described as following:

When a user chooses a period from dropdown 

  • It should display opening balance ie., it should consider all periods before the user selected period 
  •  It should display closing balance ie., it should consider all periods up to the selected period and 
  •  All other charts should display values only for that selected period.

Approach Used:

SAC scripting is used in OnInitialization event to retrieve selected period and apply necessary changes on the charts to display opening and closing balances based on selected period when the dashboard is loading.

Implementation

The code  written inside the OnInitialization event ensures that filtering happens automatically in the backend.

Step 1 – Dropdown Selection and OnInitialization logic

When the user selects a period from the dropdown, the script retrieves the selected value and applies filters to the charts accordingly.The selected key gets stored in a variable and applied as a filter.

Scriptvariable_Getkey=Dropdown_1.getSelectedKey();
Chart_2.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Getkey);

The script then retrieves all members available in the POSTING_YEAR dimension and converts string to numbers to achieve proper comparison.

Scriptvariable_Memberinfo=Chart_2.getDataSource().getMembers("POSTING_YEAR");
Scriptvariable_Number=ConvertUtils.stringToNumber(Scriptvariable_Getkey);

In this step the script identifies all the periods before the selected period to and applies the filtered periods on Chart_2 to retrieve Opening balance.

for (var i = 0; i < Scriptvariable_Memberinfo.length; i++) {
 var year = ConvertUtils.stringToNumber(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[i].id); 
if (year < Scriptvariable_Number) { Scriptvariable_Filter.push(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[i].id); 
} 
}
Chart_2.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Filter);

To calculate closing balance in this step the script retrieves all the period up to the selected period and applies the filtered period on Chart_3.

for (var l = 0; l < Scriptvariable_Memberinfo.length; l++) { 
var year1 = ConvertUtils.stringToNumber(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[l].id); 
if (year1 <= Scriptvariable_Number) { Scriptvariable_Filter1.push(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[l].id);
 } 
}
Chart_3.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Filter1);

All other charts are filtered only for the selected period.

Chart_9.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Getkey); 
Chart_10.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Getkey);

Step 2 – Default Behaviour on Page Load

Initially when the dashboard gets loaded in dropdown it shows the current year and month will be in the dropdown to enhance usability.The below script retrieves current month and year and formats accordingly.

Scriptvariable_CurrentYear = (new Date()).getFullYear().toString(); Scriptvariable_Currentmonth=(new Date()).getMonth().toString();
ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum=ConvertUtils.stringToInteger(Scriptvariable_Currentmonth); ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum1=ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum+1; ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr=ConvertUtils.numberToString(ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum1);
ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr1=('0'+ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr).slice(-2);
Scriptvariable_Combine=Scriptvariable_CurrentYear+ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr1;

 The below script ensures that users always see the recent financial data immediately after opening the dashboard.

Dropdown_1.setSelectedKey(Scriptvariable_Combine);

Conclusion:

Dynamic reporting is necessary especially for a financial dashboard where the requirement is to automatically show opening and closing balances without any manual workload . SAC scripting approach simplifies the process and improves efficiency and enhances the user experience.

Output :

 Dynamic Dropdown for Period-Based Filtering

Dynamic Balance Calculation Based on Selected Period

entry.title: Dynamic Filtering in SAC using Logged in user details to show organization level data
entry.content:
Introduction :

In SAC a common requirement is to filter data automatically based on the logged-in user. SAC makes this relatively straightforward because the logged-in User ID can be retrieved directly through scripting.

In our scenario, the requirement was slightly different users should not see only their own records, but rather all the records belonging to their organization. To achieve this requirement we need to first get the organization details of the logged in user then pass it as a filter across the story.

This blog will explain how this requirement shall be implemented using scripting in SAC and hidden helper charts.

Business requirement :

The dashboard is used by users from multiple organizations.

So when the user opens the dashboard:

  1. The user information need to be identified.
  2. Then we need to find the organization associated with the user.
  3. The dashboard should display all the records related to the users organization.
  4. User should be able to see the records related to other users of the same organization.

    Approach used:

    You may think why can’t we directly filter by user ID for this scenario, we could filter the dashboard using the user ID but doing that it would only show the data related to that particular user we won’t be able to show the records of all other users of the same organization.

    So to achieve this,

    1. We first get the logged in user ID.
    2. Retrieve the organization associated with the user.
    3. Apply that organization as filter to the dashboard.
    4. In addition to that we have also dynamically populated the dropdown based on the filtered data.

      To achieve this we shall use 2 helper charts in the story. These charts will be hidden in the dashboard and will be only used to retrieve data through scripting.

      Chart 1 - is used to retrieve the organization associated with the user.

      Chart 2 : Retrieves the user belonging to the filtered organization.

      Code :

      The code is written in the onIntialization event of the SAC story

      1. Retrieve the ID of the user and pass it as filter on Chart 1 to retrieve the relevant records.

var1 = Application.getUserInfo().id;
Chart_1.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("PR_CREATED_BY",var1);

2. After applying the filter we retrieve the result set and get the user organization.

var2 = Chart_1.getDataSource().getResultSet();
for (var i = 0; i < var2.length; i++) {
var3.push(var2[i]["Organisation"].id);
}

3. Populate the dropdown

Dropdown_1.removeAllItems();
for (var j = 0; j < var3.length; j++) {
Dropdown_1.addItem(var3[j]);
}

4. Applying the organization filter to the table and retrieving the user based on organization.

Table_4.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Organisation", var3);
Chart_2.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Organisation",var3);
var5 = Chart_2.getDataSource().getResultSet();
for (var k = 0; k < var5.length; k++) {
var6.push(var5[k]["PR_CREATED_BY"].id);
}

Therefore through the above approach the user can see the organisation level details.

Output Scenarios :
1. When one user is associated with more than one organization all those organization will get populated in the dropdown and same will be shown in table


2. The dashboard displays all records belonging to the user’s organization, allowing users to view data created by other users within the same organization.



3. Hidden charts that are used to fetch user organization and user id details

entry.content:

For one of my dashboard requirement, i need to calculate the date difference between two events but the date appears of 2 event in same column but in 2 different rows. for example, i want to calculate the difference between hold and resume time of SFC. SFC production events MDO has this time in EVentoccuredat column but as 2 rows differentiated by Eventtype as "SFC_HOLD" and "SFC_HOLD_RELEASED". does anyone have an idea to calculate this duration.

MS

entry.title: Breaking into SAP BTP
entry.content:
How can I learn about SAP?

One of the questions asked at our last networking event came from a master’s student who is interested in learning about SAP technology. It was quite a simple one - “How can I learn about SAP?”. 

While simple, her question has a far more complex answer beyond just “It depends…”. SAP is enormous - different products, different technologies, different disciplines. With SAP’s 50+years of history of creating complex business systems, where do you even start?

For myself, I got into SAP in the same way as most people I’ve met seem to - my boss made me learn it. One day my 20-year-old self was working on an old SQR program when my manager walked in and told me I would be learning SAP and sent me to the office next door where an implementation of SAP 3.1h was ongoing. It was, of course, the first time I had heard of SAP (or for that matter that an implementation was ongoing) and on arrival I met the consultant team who taught me ABAP, which I then worked in for the next two decades.

But if you’re not blessed with accidentally stumbling into a life changing career, how can you learn SAP technologies and be reasonably confident that an employer will value the effort you’ve put into learning it? In this blog I’m going to try and give some pointers as to how this can be accomplished. Just to put some kind of scope on this blog, we’ll be concentrating on SAP BTP.

 

Step 1 - Understand what it is

SAP BTP is the foundation technology SAP has created to act as their integrated "platform-as-a-service" (PaaS). Its primary role is to enable the "clean core" architecture and move customers away from building applications directly inside the SAP core.

The Four Functional Pillars

SAP BTP is organized into four distinct areas, each of which fulfils a specific role.

  • Application Development: These are the tools for professional developers (using the CAP model and BAS) and business users (using SAP Build) to create applications.
  • Integration: The SAP Integration Suite is a set of tools that allow BTP to connect to both SAP and on-SAP systems. In here you’ll find things such as Event Mesh, Destination services, technical adaptors (OData, SOAP, Rest etc.) and everything else you’ll need to plug systems into the BTP landscape.
  • Data and Analytics: This is where raw data is transformed into real information. It includes SAP HANA Cloud for data storage and SAP Analytics Cloud for visualization.
  • Artificial Intelligence: SAP BTP is where Joule, SAP’s generative AI copilot, exists. It provides SAP’s pre-built AI services for document processing, sentiment analysis, and recommendation engines that are already embedded into business workflows.

To learn these in more depth than can be put on a blog post read the "Discover SAP Business Technology Platform" learning journey on the sap learning portal. It covers these four pillars and can be found here.

https://learning.sap.com/courses/discovering-sap-business-technology-platform-1?searchId=7d314e8c-19cc-4f14-97ff-0570f56ab26d&listPosition=1

 

Step 2 - Get your hands dirty

Setup a BTP trial account

Reading up about BTP is useful, but nothing beats practical experience. SAP has free BTP trial accounts where you can sign up to a 90-day trial. It provides access to the BTP Cockpit where you can enable services like SAP Build, SAP HANA Cloud, and Integration Suite.

https://www.sap.com/products/technology-platform/trial.html#:~:text=You%20can%20choose%20a%20tutorial,services%20in%20a%20test%20environment.

From my own experience, it’s not for the faint of heart or non-technical among us as you need to do a fair amount of work to configure the system and learn how it operates. However, if you want to learn BTP admin setup, technical skills and eventually start building applications – then this is for you.

 

Explore the SAP Discovery Center

In addition to the SAP learning portal, SAP has created the discovery center. In it you will find “missions” which are step-by-step projects that walk you through specific use cases. It’s fully searchable by topic and has quite a lot of useful content.

Looking up SAP Joule, for example, shows 16 missions ranging from getting started with SAP Build and Joule (using that trial account you’ve surely setup by now) to integrating SAP Joule with Microsoft Co-Pilot.

The link can be found here https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/

 

Step 3 - Understand what you want to do

SAP BTP is too broad for any one person to master. Pick a track based on your role – or the role that you want. A few examples are below. This is by no means an exhaustive list and you may see yourself fitting into a different category altogether.

Track

Tools

Core Skills

Developer

CAP (Cloud Application Programming), SAPUI5, Node.js, Java

Building side-by-side extensions for S/4HANA, understanding the clean-core approach.

Integrator

SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration, API Management)

Connecting SAP to non-SAP systems (e.g., Salesforce, ServiceNow).

Low-Code/No Code

SAP Build (Apps, Process Automation, Work Zone)

Creating apps and workflows without deep coding knowledge

Data & AI

SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP AI Core

Managing enterprise data and deploying AI models.

A subject that came up during our discussions (and which tends to be overlooked) is that knowledge matters. A financial expert with a grounding in accounting will be able to create and utilise finance based low-code solutions far more efficiently than someone who is relying on the machine to do it for them.

This is not going to go away in the age of LLMs and AI. Someone who knows how a business process works or what the subject theory is will be able to spot hallucinations, ask far more pertinent questions and direct outcomes more efficiently and with better results. Don’t neglect the grounding knowledge that will allow you to both use and master these tools.

 Advanced Technical Skills

This blog could only be about technical tools and skills, and I’d likely be writing it forever.  But in the interest of creating a shortlist, here are a few of the technical skills that standout.

  • Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP): This is where SAP wants developers to concentrate their efforts in BTP development. Core Data Services (CDS) are crucial to define data models and learning this route. For those of you who want to eventually sell products directly on the SAP store, then here’s where you do it.
  • SAP Business Application Studio (BAS): This is the cloud-based IDE (successor to Web IDE) where most BTP development happens. It has replaced the SAP Web IDE and is the primary tool SAP has provided for creating those side-by-side extensions. If you are just starting, focus entirely on BAS and avoid Web IDE. The SAP BTP trial can be used to access it and use the CAP (Cloud Application Programming) model within BAS.
  • Joule-Powered Development The biggest shift I’ve seen in the last while is the new Agent Builder and Joule Studio integration. You can now use natural language within BAS to generate CAP (Cloud Application Programming) models, UI5 code, and even specialized "AI Agents" that automate business processes. While this is still in the early stages, I’m looking forward to seeing what SAP brings us with this toolset.

 

Step 4 - Certification

At TechEd 2025, we learned that the certification process that SAP have employed for decades was being turned on its head. Previously, a consultant had to memorise volumes of information and then sit a multi-choice exam on the topic. My own, perhaps controversial, opinion was that this approach only ever proved that the consultant could do an exam - not the job. I never valued certification for this reason.

SAP has transitioned to a more performance-based certification model. Here, the would-be consultant is given an open book exam with problems to solve. It reflects the real-world scenario where we have the entire internet, custom AI agents and Joule for Consultants at our fingertips, not to mention other experts on the team you can rely on. Someone who has gone through this very different approach to getting certified needs to be able to problem solve and rapidly adjust to new realities. This type of certification would certainly push someone higher on my list of potential candidates to hire.

Just to note, while many courses are free, a paid subscription to the Learning Hub is required for official certification attempts and access to "Live Access" training systems.

https://learning.sap.com/certifications?page=1

Ultimately there is no “one route” into SAP and it takes time and effort to become an expert in any one area. Hopefully, however, this blog post will give you some guidance on where to start and how to learn some useful skills that an employer will value.

entry.title: Re: BAPI_PO_CHANGE meaning of X key fields of the X table parameters

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent (Part 1 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 1 OF 6
SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent

What agentic AI actually means in SAP contexts, when to use an agent vs a skill vs a pipeline, the full agentic SAP stack with LangGraph and AI Core, and a working ReAct agent for SAP data — all in Part 1.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 1 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Agentic AI is not just a more capable pipeline. It's a fundamentally different execution model — the agent decides what to do next based on what it observes, rather than following a fixed sequence. This article covers what that means for SAP workloads specifically, when agents are the right choice vs skills and pipelines, the complete LangGraph + AI Core architecture for production SAP agents, and a working ReAct agent that queries SAP data, analyzes it, and produces findings autonomously.

Series 2 Context — Building on the Generative AI Hub Foundation

This is Part 1 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice — the second series building on the Generative AI Hub foundation. Series 1 covered setup, Python SDK, prompt pipelines, RAG on HANA Cloud, CAP applications, and Joule Studio. If you haven't read that series, the architecture knowledge from Parts 3–5 will be particularly useful background for this one.

Series 2 moves from pipelines to agents. The distinction matters — and Part 1 establishes it clearly before writing a single line of agent code. The four SAP use cases this series builds toward are: data migration landscape assessment, exception log triage, master data quality, and S/4HANA readiness. Each becomes a specialized agent. By Part 6 they work together as a coordinated multi-agent system.

PART A: CONCEPTS — WHAT AGENTIC AI ACTUALLY MEANS 

Agent vs Skill vs Pipeline — The Distinction That Matters

These three terms are used interchangeably in most SAP AI conversations. They shouldn't be. They represent fundamentally different execution models with different appropriate use cases.

Type

How It Works

SAP Example

Prompt Pipeline

Fixed sequence of prompts — output of one feeds the next. You define every step upfront. The model executes but doesn't decide.

Exception log triage with defined classify → summarize → recommend steps. Predictable, auditable, fast.

Joule Skill

Structured, deterministic capability triggered by user intent. Calls a specific API action. No reasoning or planning involved.

Answering a knowledge base question, looking up a material, creating a badge. Single-purpose, reliable.

AI Agent

Autonomous reasoner. Given a goal, the agent decides which tools to use, in what order, based on what it observes at each step. The sequence is not predetermined.

Assess an SAP landscape for S/4HANA readiness — the agent decides which systems to query, what to look for, and how to structure findings based on what it discovers.

The key differentiator is decision-making under uncertainty. Pipelines and skills are deterministic — given the same input, they always follow the same path. Agents are adaptive — they observe, reason, and decide what to do next based on what they find.

When to Use Each

Use a pipeline when the steps are known upfront and the output structure is fixed. Use a skill when the task is focused and the API call is well-defined. Use an agent when the task requires exploration, multi-step reasoning, or when the path to completion depends on what the agent discovers along the way.

Scenario

Use

SAP Example

Known steps, fixed output structure

Prompt pipeline

Exception log triage, document extraction

Single focused task, defined API

Joule skill

Material lookup, KB question, badge creation

Goal-directed, path unknown upfront

Agent

Landscape assessment, readiness analysis

Multi-step with dependencies on findings

Agent

Data quality sweep, migration risk scoring

Needs human approval at checkpoints

Agent with human-in-the-loop

Transport risk assessment, SoD conflict review

Agents are not always better than pipelines. They are more expensive (more LLM calls), less predictable (the path varies), and harder to debug. Use agents specifically when the task requires adaptive reasoning. For everything else, pipelines and skills are faster, cheaper, and more reliable.

PART B: ARCHITECTURE — THE AGENTIC SAP STACK 

The Complete LangGraph + AI Core Architecture for SAP Agents

Production SAP agents require four layers working together. LangGraph provides the agent orchestration framework. SAP AI Core provides governed foundation model access. HANA Cloud provides the data and vector layer. BTP Cloud Foundry provides the deployment and runtime environment.

LangGraph Agent → AI Core (foundation model) → SAP Tools (HANA, APIs) → HANA vector store → Findings output
Why LangGraph for SAP Agents

LangGraph is a graph-based agent orchestration framework built on LangChain. It models agent execution as a directed graph — nodes are actions or decisions, edges are transitions. This architecture makes it uniquely suited for SAP workloads for three reasons:

  • State persistence — LangGraph maintains agent state explicitly across steps. For SAP assessments that query multiple systems and accumulate findings, state persistence is essential.
  • Conditional routing — edges in the graph can be conditional. An agent can take different paths based on what it finds — escalate a critical finding, skip a check if a prerequisite fails, loop back for more data if confidence is low.
  • Human-in-the-loop support — LangGraph has native support for pausing execution and waiting for human approval before proceeding. For SAP processes where autonomous action carries risk, this is not optional.
The ReAct Pattern

ReAct (Reason + Act) is the foundational agent pattern used throughout this series. The agent alternates between two steps:

  • Reason — the model thinks about what it knows and what it needs to do next
  • Act — the model calls a tool (a function, an API, a database query) and observes the result

This cycle continues until the agent has enough information to produce a final answer or reaches a defined stopping condition. For SAP workloads, the tools are SAP-specific: HANA queries, RFC calls, AI Core model invocations, vector store retrievals.

Goal input → Reason: what do I know? → Act: call SAP tool → Observe result → Reason: what next? → Final findings
Agent State Schema for SAP Assessments

Every LangGraph agent requires an explicit state schema. For SAP assessment agents, the state carries the accumulated findings, queries run, systems checked, and confidence scores across all steps:

# Agent state schema — carries context across all reasoning steps

from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

class SAPAssessmentState(TypedDict):

    goal:              str          # What the agent is trying to assess

    system_info:       dict         # SAP system metadata collected so far

    findings:          List[dict]   # Accumulated findings with severity

    queries_run:       List[str]    # Queries executed — for audit trail

    tools_used:        List[str]    # Tools called — for transparency

    confidence_score:  float        # Overall confidence in findings

    next_action:       str          # What the agent decided to do next

    human_review_needed: bool       # Flag for human-in-the-loop checkpoint

    final_report:      Optional[str] # Final output when complete
PART C: YOUR FIRST SAP AGENT — REACT WITH LANGGRAPH 

Building a ReAct Agent for SAP Data Assessment

This first agent demonstrates the complete ReAct pattern on a focused SAP task: given a HANA table name, autonomously assess its data quality — checking volume, null rates, duplicate rates, and anomalies — and produce a structured findings report. This is the foundation pattern that the more complex agents in Parts 2–6 build on.

Step 1 — Install Dependencies
# Install LangGraph and SAP AI SDK
pip install langgraph langchain-core generative-ai-hub-sdk hdbcli
Step 2 — Define SAP Tools

Tools are the functions the agent can call. Each tool has a name, description (used by the model to decide when to call it), and implementation. For SAP agents, tools wrap HANA queries and AI Core calls:

# tools.py — SAP tools available to the agent
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json
 
# HANA connection — credentials from environment or .aicore-config.json
def get_hana_connection():
    return hdb.connect(
        address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'],
        port=443,
        user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
        password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'],
        encrypt=True
    )
 
@tool
def get_table_volume(table_name: str) -> str:
    """Get the row count and basic metadata for an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table_name}')
    count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
    return json.dumps({'table': table_name, 'row_count': count})
 
@tool
def get_null_rate(table_name: str, column_name: str) -> str:
    """Calculate the null rate for a specific column in an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            COUNT(*) as total,
            SUM(CASE WHEN {column_name} IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as nulls,
            ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN {column_name} IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
                / COUNT(*), 2) as null_rate_pct
        FROM {table_name}
    ''')
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    return json.dumps({
        'table': table_name, 'column': column_name,
        'total': row[0], 'nulls': row[1], 'null_rate_pct': float(row[2])
    })
 
@tool
def get_duplicate_rate(table_name: str, key_column: str) -> str:
    """Identify duplicate records based on a key column in an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT {key_column}) as duplicate_count,
               COUNT(*) as total_count
        FROM {table_name}
    ''')
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    return json.dumps({
        'table': table_name, 'key_column': key_column,
        'duplicate_count': row[0], 'total_count': row[1],
        'duplicate_rate_pct': round(100.0 * row[0] / row[1], 2) if row[1] > 0 else 0
    })
 
@tool
def generate_assessment_report(findings: str) -> str:
    """Generate a structured data quality assessment report from accumulated findings."""
    from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
    from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
    llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
    response = llm([
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP data quality expert.
        Generate a structured assessment report from the findings provided.
        Include: Executive Summary, Critical Issues, Recommendations.
        Be specific and actionable.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Generate report from findings:\n{findings}')
    ])
    return response.content

Step 3 — Build the LangGraph Agent

# agent.py — LangGraph ReAct agent for SAP data quality assessment
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from tools import get_table_volume, get_null_rate, get_duplicate_rate, generate_assessment_report
 
# Initialize foundation model via SAP AI Core
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
# Register SAP tools the agent can use
tools = [get_table_volume, get_null_rate, get_duplicate_rate, generate_assessment_report]
 
# System prompt — defines agent behavior and boundaries
system_prompt = '''You are an SAP data quality assessment agent.
Your goal is to autonomously assess the data quality of SAP HANA tables.

For each assessment:
1. Check the table volume first
2. Check null rates for key columns
3. Check duplicate rates on primary key columns
4. Flag any issue where null_rate > 5% or duplicate_rate > 0.1% as HIGH severity
5. Generate a structured findings report when assessment is complete

Always explain your reasoning before calling a tool.
Always summarize what you found after each tool call.
Stop when you have assessed all requested dimensions.'''
 
# Create the ReAct agent — LangGraph handles the reason/act loop
agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=tools,
    state_modifier=system_prompt
)

Step 4 — Run the Agent

# Run the agent on an SAP table assessment task
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
 
result = agent.invoke({
    'messages': [HumanMessage(content='''
        Assess the data quality of table MARA (SAP material master).
        Check volume, null rate on MTART and MATKL columns,
        and duplicate rate on MATNR. Generate a findings report.
    ''')]
})
 
# Print the agent's final response
print(result['messages'][-1].content)

The agent will reason through the task step by step — calling get_table_volume first, then get_null_rate for each column, then get_duplicate_rate, and finally generate_assessment_report with all accumulated findings. The reasoning trace is visible in the messages — each HumanMessage and AIMessage shows exactly what the agent thought and did.

What the Agent Output Looks Like

The agent produces a structured findings report with:

  • Executive Summary — overall data quality score and critical issues count
  • Findings per dimension — volume, nulls, duplicates with actual values
  • Severity classification — Critical, High, Medium, Low per finding
  • Specific recommendations — actionable fixes referencing actual column names and values

This output is the foundation for the migration assessment agent in Part 2, which extends this pattern across multiple SAP tables and produces a full landscape readiness report.

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         SAP DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT AGENT — EXECUTION TRACE      ║
║         Table: MARA (Material Master — General Data)             ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

[THOUGHT] I need to begin by checking the volume of the MARA table to
understand the scale of the assessment before evaluating data quality
dimensions.

[ACTION] get_table_volume("MARA")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "row_count": 847293}

[THOUGHT] The table contains 847,293 material master records. This is
a significant volume — quality issues even at low percentages will
affect thousands of records. I'll now check null rates starting with
MTART (Material Type), a critical classification field.

[ACTION] get_null_rate("MARA", "MTART")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "column": "MTART", "total": 847293,
               "nulls": 169, "null_rate_pct": 0.02}

[THOUGHT] MTART null rate is 0.02% — acceptable. 169 records without
a material type is low risk. Moving to MATKL (Material Group), which
drives procurement categorization and reporting.

[ACTION] get_null_rate("MARA", "MATKL")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "column": "MATKL", "total": 847293,
               "nulls": 73,969, "null_rate_pct": 8.73}

[THOUGHT] MATKL null rate is 8.73% — this exceeds the 5% threshold
and is flagged HIGH severity. 73,969 materials without a material group
will cause issues in procurement reporting, spend analytics, and any
AI classification pipeline that relies on MATKL for categorization.
This needs to be highlighted in the final report.

[ACTION] get_duplicate_rate("MARA", "MATNR")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "key_column": "MATNR",
               "duplicate_count": 0, "total_count": 847293,
               "duplicate_rate_pct": 0.0}

[THOUGHT] MATNR (Material Number) has zero duplicates — primary key
integrity is intact. I now have sufficient findings to generate the
structured assessment report.

[ACTION] generate_assessment_report(findings)

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SAP DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT REPORT
Table: MARA — Material Master General Data
Assessment Date: 2026-03-28 | Agent: SAP Data Quality Agent v1.0
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
─────────────────
Overall Data Quality Score: 74/100 — ATTENTION REQUIRED
Records Assessed: 847,293
Critical Issues: 0  |  High Severity: 1  |  Low Severity: 2

One high-severity data quality issue was identified that will impact
procurement reporting, spend analytics, and downstream AI pipelines
that rely on material group classification. Immediate remediation is
recommended before any S/4HANA migration or AI workload deployment.

FINDINGS
────────
┌──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────┬──────────┬──────────┐
│ Dimension    │ Column       │ Value         │ Threshold│ Severity │
├──────────────┼──────────────┼───────────────┼──────────┼──────────┤
│ Volume       │ —            │ 847,293 rows  │ —        │ OK       │
│ Null Rate    │ MTART        │ 0.02% (169)   │ < 5%     │ LOW      │
│ Null Rate    │ MATKL        │ 8.73% (73,969)│ < 5%     │ HIGH ⚠   │
│ Duplicates   │ MATNR        │ 0.0%          │ < 0.1%   │ LOW      │
└──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────┴──────────┴──────────┘

HIGH SEVERITY FINDING — MATKL (Material Group) Null Rate: 8.73%
73,969 of 847,293 material records are missing a Material Group
assignment. Impact areas:
  • Procurement spend reporting by category will be incomplete
  • AI-based material classification will produce unreliable results
  • S/4HANA migration data validation will flag these records

RECOMMENDATIONS
───────────────
1. [IMMEDIATE] Run MATKL gap analysis — extract all MATNR records
   where MATKL is null. Group by MTART to identify which material
   types are most affected. This scopes the remediation effort.

2. [SHORT-TERM] Assign Material Groups using the AI classification
   pipeline (Part 2 of Generative AI Hub series) — embed existing
   classified materials as reference examples and classify the
   73,969 unclassified records using RAG-augmented classification.

3. [BEFORE MIGRATION] Validate MATKL completeness reaches < 2%
   null rate before proceeding with any S/4HANA migration data
   load. Include this check in your migration go/no-go criteria.

4. [PROCESS] Add MATKL as a required field in material master
   creation workflows in MM01 to prevent future gaps.

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Agent completed assessment in 4 reasoning steps | 4 tool calls
Confidence Score: 0.91 | Human Review Required: NO
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SERIES 2 ROADMAP 

What This Series Builds Across 6 Parts

 

Part

Title

What It Builds

Part 1

Concepts, Architecture, First ReAct Agent

Agent vs skill vs pipeline, LangGraph + AI Core stack, data quality ReAct agent on HANA

Part 2

SAP Data Migration Assessment Agent

Multi-tool agent that analyzes SAP landscape, scores migration readiness, produces findings report

Part 3

SAP Exception Log Triage Agent

Autonomous root cause analysis — agent queries logs, identifies patterns, recommends actions without human triage

Part 4

SAP Master Data Quality Agent

Detects data issues across master data domains, prioritizes fixes, generates remediation plan

Part 5

S/4HANA Readiness Agent

Assesses custom code and configuration against clean core principles, flags migration blockers

Part 6

Multi-Agent Orchestration

All four agents working together — an orchestrator agent delegates to specialized sub-agents and synthesizes a unified SAP landscape report

What's Next — Part 2

Part 2 builds the SAP Data Migration Assessment Agent — extending the ReAct pattern from a single table to a full landscape assessment across multiple systems, with conditional routing based on findings and a scored readiness report as output. The agent decides autonomously which systems to query, what to look for, and how to weight findings based on migration impact.

If you work through the ReAct agent in this article and want to extend it to your own SAP tables — drop a comment with your use case. The pattern generalizes across any HANA table and any assessment dimension. The tool definitions are the only thing that changes.

entry.title: Cloud Integration Pipeline - Datastore Extension UI
entry.content:
⚠️  Disclaimer: The UI described in this blog post is not an official product by SAP. If you are interested in using the UI, you can contact @lisarizzo or @mike_sibler.  It will soon also be available as an SAP Open Source project.  

 

Purpose and background

The pipeline in Cloud Integration is a well-established framework for asynchronous integrations. A blog post to explain the starting points for the pipeline can be found here.

In addition to the pipeline, we offer the datastore extension, which simplifies error handling via automatic retry jobs and APIs for resending and cancelling messages. For details on the datastore extension, please refer to this blog post. The datastore extension package provides automatic restart capabilities with Restart Jobs that are based on pre-delivered IFlows. In addition, it provides on-demand operations that require the use of API client tools such as Postman or Bruno. This is technical and error-prone to handle from a user perspective.

The UI introduced in this blog post is designed to provide a simplified and user-friendly approach to access these on-demand APIs.

On a technical level, it is relevant to note that the UI does not call Cloud Integration APIs directly. It calls IFlows of the Datastore Extension package, which are on-demand API Operations (Manage Retries and Manage Datastore Entries). It triggers a corresponding IFlow and operation and then displays the response.

This blog article introduces and explains the functionality of the Cloud Integration Pipeline Datastore Extension UI.

 

Overview of key features

The current version of the UI provides these capabilities, which are shown again in more detail in the “How to use the UI” section.

  • Visual overview of all available datastores relevant for the pipeline reprocessing, their messages and overdue messages
  • Search and filter capabilities to quickly locate specific datastores
  • Visual overview of all messages in each datastore
  • Search and filter capabilities to quickly locate specific messages
  • Action to restart messages from the UI
  • Action to move messages to another datastore or a no-retry datastore when located in a retry datastore
  • Action to delete/cancel messages 
  • Detailed view of message content (error, headers, payload)
  • Possibility to perform batch actions to restart, move, cancel several messages at once

 

How to use the UI

Datastore overview

The first screen of the UI shows a table with all the datastores, the number of messages, and the number of overdue messages. You can also search and filter the table for specific information. 

Datastore detail view and overview of messages

The second screen shows details of the selected datastore and contains a table with all the messages. 

One or more messages can be selected in the table and moved to another datastore or to a no-retry datastore. The messages can also be restarted or deleted from here.  

There are sorting capabilities available for the datastore entries table. They can be found behind the gear icon ⚙️. For example, the table can be sorted by the number of restarts and the timestamp of error, ascending or descending. 

There is also a filter hidden behind the same gear icon ⚙️.

Message detail view

When clicking on one of the rows in the datastore entries table the user is directed to a third screen with details about the error, headers and payload. If, after inspecting, the user decided what to do with the message it is also possible to perform the same move, delete and restart actions from this screen with the buttons in the top right corner.

 

Outlook & how to implement the UI

This project will soon be available on the SAP GitHub. Until then, for information on how to implement the Datastore Extension UI, please reach out to lisa.rizzo@sap.com or mike.sibler@sap.com . Once the project is published on the SAP GitHub as an open-source project, you can find the link to the repository and a detailed implementation guide here in this blog post.

entry.title: Re: The Ultimate Guide to Product Costing in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Après une phase de pré-projet pour un groupe industriel français du secteur métallurgique, et après une année de collaboration avec des consultants et intégrateurs externes, juste avant le lancement de l'intégration SAP S/4HANA, nous avons participé a la présentation du projet de bout en bout.

Le texte ci-dessus illustre parfaitement les différentes étapes, mais j'apprécie tout particulièrement la présentation et la synthèse de l'objectif des actions menées dans SAP, et de leur finalité.

 

Souvent, dans la multitude d'opérations à accomplir, nous perdons de vue le sens et les objectifs.

 

Un fait marquant : le processus complexe – c’est-à-dire la production manufacturière dans différentes usines et différents lieux géographiques

#SAP  #METALURGIE #PRIXREVIENT

entry.title: Re: Request to Change the IT Contact Person
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Kindly know the IT contact person is the person who is dedicated in the cloud contract. This contact person cannot be maintained or updated somewhere in SAP for Me.

If the current IT contact person is outdated and need to be changed, could you pls kindly contact your SAP Account Manager to verify the IT contact person from the contract point of view? and then your account manager will work with the Order Management/Operation team to update the IT contact person if necessary.

If you have no idea who your SAP Account Manager is, kindly refer to KBA 2626344 and contact SAP CIC support team.

Hope this above information helps and sorry for the inconvenience.

Thanks for your understanding and best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Compensation salary - Mandatory Compensation Adjustme...
entry.content:
I have implemented this requirement several times.   The best way to do this is to use a guideline that either indicates the appropriate amount or percentage as a default.   Because it is mandatory, you should make the field read only.

If only certain people should get the increase, i.e. a certain country or pay band, then you should implement an eligibility rule to only have those eligible people on the form.

If this process occurs multiple times throughout the year, you would have to launch new forms each time.

entry.title: Re: Unable to capture "Substitute" assignments for email...
entry.content:
Hello @GauravSurwade ,

This is a common challenge in SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) integrated with My Inbox. By default, BPA workflows are not automatically aware of substitutes set in My Inbox, so custom notifications are sent to the original approver.

Using a Distribution List (DL) in Outlook can be a practical workaround. By sending notifications to a group email address (DL) that includes both the original approver and potential substitutes, you ensure that someone in the group receives the notification, even if the substitute changes.

However, please note that this approach does not dynamically reflect the actual substitute set in SAP My Inbox—it’s a manual group. Alternatively, you can use the “Send E-mail” option before forwarding or delegating the task.

 

I hope it helps ,Thanks,

Vennilla

entry.title: FIN Travel Management
entry.content:
#FIN Travel Management

 

Hello, experts!

We have below mentioned days and hours maintained in table T706V (Define Per Diem for Meals), where we have ANSTD = 24, but sometimes when users create travel report through fiori Per Diem calculation is wrong.

For example, travel for 3 days. There is Per Diem for 3 days calculation:

2 days sum = 2* T706V-BETFA ( and T706V-BEGDA = 01012026 and T706V-ENDDA = 31129999).

1 day sum = 1* T706V-BETFA (and T706V-BEGDA = 01012025 and T706V-ENDDA = 31122025).

If we add additional similar entry for  T706V-BEGDA = 01012026 and T706V-ENDDA = 31129999, but T706V-ANSTD = 25 the problem will be solved

and calculation 3 days will be = 3*T706V-BETFA ( and T706V-BEGDA = 01012026 and T706V-ENDDA = 31129999).

 

Please, could you explain me why  or what I do wrong?

 

Regards, Hanna

entry.title: Why Employee termination date is not getting updated in SAP Concur (ICS integration)
entry.content:
Query: Why is the termination date not getting updated in SAP Concur for the employees already terminated in SAP S/4 HANA system while using SAP ICS integration?

Solution: Sometimes while using the SAP ICS integration for Employee Replication from SAP S/4 HANA system to SAP Concur system, the employee’s termination date is not updated in SAP Concur system and their SAP Concur profile stays active though the employee left the company.

There could be several reasons which can causes of this issue. Below are few troubleshooting points which can help to fix this problem.

Employees termination date will be sent from SAP S/4 HANA system to SAP Concur system in the below cases:

  • The employee’s employment status in the SAP S/4 HANA system must be inactive.
  • The employee must already be part of the successful replicated employees from SAP S/4 HANA system to SAP Concur system in past.
  • The employee must be terminated in the SAP S/4 HANA system, and the retention period must be maintained per country/region to export the employee’s termination date to SAP Concur system.
  • The employee replication ‘Specify Field Mapping’ has the ‘Export Enabled” button ticked (Export=YES) for the field mapping of the field termination date.
  • The employee’s termination date must be picked and filled with a correct value in JSON file generated for Employee Replication from SAP S/4 HANA system to SAP Concur system.
